title: seizeAllowed is dead code — global seize pause has no effect and seize guard is entirely bypassed
claim: The Comptroller.seizeAllowed() function is defined with security checks (global seize pause, credit account protection, market listing verification, comptroller matching) but is never called anywhere in the seize flow. The BToken.seize() function directly calls seizeInternal() without invoking comptroller.seizeAllowed(), rendering the _setSeizePaused() global pause and all seizeAllowed checks completely non-functional.
impact: During an emergency, the guardian or admin cannot pause collateral seizures globally via _setSeizePaused(). The seizeGuardianPaused flag is never read in any execution path. Additionally, the comptroller-matching check (ensuring both borrowed and collateral BTokens share the same comptroller) and the credit-account seizure protection in seizeAllowed are never enforced, though partial duplicate checks exist in liquidateBorrowAllowed.

paths.description: Guardian sets global seize pause but seizures continue unaffected
steps: 1. Guardian or admin calls _setSeizePaused(true) to globally pause all collateral seizures during an emergency
2. A liquidator calls liquidateBorrow() on a BToken
3. liquidateBorrowFresh() internally calls repayBorrowFresh() then calls bTokenCollateral.seize() or seizeInternal()
4. The seize() function calls seizeInternal() directly without checking comptroller.seizeAllowed()
5. Collateral is seized successfully despite the global pause being active
6. The seizeGuardianPaused flag is never consulted — the emergency pause is completely ineffective

title: _setCloseFactor allows close factor of 0% or >100% with no bounds validation
claim: The _setCloseFactor() admin function has no validation on newCloseFactorMantissa. The close factor can be set to 0 (disabling all liquidations entirely) or to values exceeding 1e18 (allowing liquidators to repay more than 100% of a borrow position in a single liquidation, potentially seizing excess collateral). On-chain the value is 1e18 (100%).
impact: An admin can accidentally or maliciously set closeFactorMantissa to 0, permanently disabling liquidations and allowing underwater positions to accumulate bad debt. Conversely, setting it above 1e18 enables a liquidator to repay more than the full borrow balance and seize disproportionately more collateral. This deviates from Compound's original design which typically caps close factor at 0.9e18 (90%) and validates the range.

paths.description: Admin sets close factor to 0, disabling all liquidations
steps: 1. Admin calls _setCloseFactor(0)
2. liquidateBorrowAllowed() computes maxClose = mul_ScalarTruncate(Exp(0), borrowBalance) = 0
3. Any repayAmount > 0 fails the 'if (repayAmount > maxClose)' check, returning TOO_MUCH_REPAY
4. All liquidation attempts revert — underwater borrowers can never be liquidated
5. Bad debt accumulates indefinitely in the protocol

paths.description: Admin sets close factor above 1e18, enabling over-liquidation
steps: 1. Admin calls _setCloseFactor(2e18) — 200% close factor
2. For a borrow of 100 ETH, maxClose = 200 ETH
3. A liquidator can repay 200 ETH of borrow and seize collateral worth 200 ETH × liquidationIncentive
4. If liquidationIncentive is also set high, the liquidator drains far more collateral than the debt repaid
5. The borrower loses disproportionate collateral

title: _setLiquidationIncentive has no bounds validation — can be set to 0 or extreme values
claim: The _setLiquidationIncentive() admin function has no validation on newLiquidationIncentiveMantissa. It can be set to 0 (liquidators receive exactly the collateral value they repay — no profit incentive to liquidate) or to extreme values (liquidators receive massive discounts on seized collateral). On-chain the value is currently 0, meaning there is zero liquidation incentive on this deployment.
impact: With liquidationIncentiveMantissa = 0 (current on-chain state), liquidators have no economic incentive to perform liquidations since they repay debt and receive collateral of equal value. This leads to a non-functional liquidation market. If set to extreme values (e.g., 2e18 = 200%), liquidators could drain collateral far exceeding the debt they repaid, causing borrower fund loss.

paths.description: Current on-chain state: liquidation incentive is 0, making liquidations unprofitable
steps: 1. On-chain state: liquidationIncentiveMantissa = 0 (verified via cast call)
2. liquidateCalculateSeizeTokens() computes seizeAmount = actualRepayAmount × 0 × priceBorrowed / priceCollateral = 0
3. Since liquidator receives 0 collateral for repaying debt, there is zero economic incentive
4. Underwater positions accumulate without anyone willing to liquidate them

paths.description: Admin sets extreme incentive enabling collateral drain
steps: 1. Admin calls _setLiquidationIncentive(3e18) — 300% incentive
2. For repaying 100 ETH of debt, liquidator seizes 300 ETH worth of collateral
3. Borrowers can be drained of all collateral even for small debt positions

title: _delistMarket can delist markets with outstanding borrows — debt becomes uncollateralized or invisible
claim: The _delistMarket() function only checks that collateralFactorMantissa == 0 and that mint/borrow/flashloan are paused, but does not check whether there are outstanding borrows in the market. After delisting: (a) soft-delisted markets retain isMarketDelisted=true so the deleted market's collateralFactorMantissa defaults to 0 in liquidity calculations, instantly under-collateralizing all borrowers; (b) hard-delisted markets are skipped entirely in getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal(), making existing borrows invisible to the liquidity system — borrowers retain the debt on the BToken but it no longer counts against their borrowing capacity in other markets.
impact: For soft delist: all borrowers with the delisted asset as collateral become eligible for liquidation since their collateral value drops to 0. For hard delist: existing borrows are 'forgiven' from a collateralization standpoint — borrowers can freely borrow against other collateral even while carrying unrepayed debt in the delisted market. This breaks the protocol's core solvency invariant.

paths.description: Hard delist causes borrows to be ignored in liquidity calculations
steps: 1. Admin sets collateralFactorMantissa to 0 for a market with active borrows
2. Admin pauses mint, borrow, and flashloan on the market
3. Admin calls _delistMarket(bToken, true) to hard-delist
4. delete markets[address(bToken)] clears the Market struct; isMarketDelisted remains false
5. getHypotheticalAccountLiquidityInternal() checks isMarketListedOrDelisted(asset) = false || false = false → skips the asset entirely via continue
6. The borrow balance in this market no longer contributes to sumBorrowPlusEffects
7. Borrower can now borrow more against other collateral despite having unpaid debt
